1: Isaiah 41:1 opens a divine courtroom scene: 'hachariyshu elai iyyim, be silent before me, O coastlands.' The charish (to be silent = the silence demanded of defendants before a court opens, cf. Habakkuk 2:20: 'let all the earth be silent before him') addresses the iyyim (coastlands = the distant Mediterranean peoples; the same term as Isaiah 40:15's 'drop in a bucket' — now summoned as defendants). 'Let the nations renew their strength; let them come near and then speak; let us together draw near for judgment' (yiggeshu az yedabberu yachdav lemishpat niqravah = they may draw near, then speak; together let us approach for judgment). The divine courtroom of Isaiah 40-48 is the literary setting in which YHWH prosecutes the case that idols are nothing and he alone is God. The 'drawing near for judgment' is YHWH's offer of legal process — not unilateral assertion but open argument.
2: 'Who has roused the conqueror from the east, whom victory meets at every step?' — the oracle points to a historical conqueror whom YHWH has mobilized. Most interpreters identify this as Cyrus II of Persia (Cyrus the Great, reigned 559-530 BCE), who conquered Media, Lydia (546 BCE under Croesus), and Babylon (539 BCE) in a remarkable sequence. Isaiah 44:28 and 45:1 name Cyrus explicitly. 'Victory meets at every step' (tsedek yiqre'enhu leraglav = righteousness/victory calls him at each footstep) = the Hebrew tsedek can mean both 'righteousness' and 'victory' — the divine righteousness meeting Cyrus at each stride makes him unstoppable. 'His sword makes them like dust, his bow like driven stubble' — the rapid devastation of Cyrus's armies: Sardis fell in two weeks (546 BCE), Babylon fell in a single battle (Opis, 539 BCE). The historical track record is the evidence YHWH presents in his courtroom.
3: 'He pursues them and passes on safely, traveling paths his feet have never trodden before' — the speed and scope of the conqueror's campaigns: 'yirdefem ya'avor shalom, he pursues them and passes on in peace.' The unusual detail that Cyrus 'passes on safely' (shalom) despite traversing unknown terrain = YHWH's protection enabling the conqueror to move through unprecedented territory unharmed. Cyrus's campaigns covered what is now Iran, Turkey, Iraq, and Syria — genuinely 'paths his feet have never trodden before' for a Persian king. The verb 'trodden' (yavo) = to come/enter — the conquered territories had never been Persian domains before. The historical novelty of Cyrus's empire — the largest that had existed to that point — is YHWH's courtroom evidence of divine orchestration.
4: 'Who has done this, calling the generations from the beginning? I, the LORD — I was first, and I am with the last; I am he' — the rhetorical question and its divine answer: 'mi-pa'al ve'asah, who has acted and done this?' The answer: 'ani YHWH ri'shon ve'et-acharonim ani-hu, I, YHWH, am the first, and with the last ones I am he.' The 'first and the last' (risohn... acharonim) formula = YHWH's existence encompasses the entire temporal span of history. He was present at creation (calling the first generation into being) and he will be present at the end (with the last). This formula is unique in the OT to Isaiah (Isaiah 44:6; Isaiah 48:12) and is taken up in Revelation 1:17; Isaiah 2:8; 22:13 for the risen Christ: 'I am the first and the last.' 'Calling the generations from the beginning' (qore haggenerot mero'sh = calling/summoning the generations from the beginning) = YHWH's sovereign summons over all of history as the one who names and calls into being.
5: 'The coastlands saw it and were afraid; the ends of the earth trembled; they drew near and came forward' — the nations' reaction to the evidence of YHWH's action through Cyrus: 'ra'u iyyim vayyira'u, the coastlands saw and were afraid.' The fear response to YHWH's power as mediated through historical events — the conquests of Cyrus that dismantled empire after empire created widespread fear among the remaining nations (the 'coastlands' and 'ends of the earth' = the most distant regions from the conquest). 'They drew near and came forward' (vayyiqrabu vayye'etayu = they drew near and came) — the drawing near is not repentance but the motion of defendants approaching the court, as commanded in v1. The irony: the nations were commanded to approach for judgment and they do so, but out of fear not contrition.
6: 'Each one helps the other, and says to his companion, Take courage!' — the mutual encouragement of the idol-worshiping nations before the divine court: 'ish et-re'ehu ya'azoru ulechachiv yomar chazaq, each man helps his companion and says to his brother: be strong!' The chazaq (be strong! = the encouragement formula used before battle, Deuteronomy 31:6-7; Joshua 1:6-9) applied ironically to idol-worshipers encouraging each other to persevere in their futile religion. The communal mutual encouragement (each one helps the other) is the social reinforcement that maintains idol-worship even when evidence against it accumulates. The contrast with v8-10 where YHWH encourages Israel ('be strong, do not fear, I will help you') is deliberate: human mutual encouragement vs. divine encouragement.
7: 'The craftsman encourages the goldsmith; the one who smooths with the hammer encourages the one striking the anvil, saying of the soldering, It is good; and they fasten it with nails so that it cannot wobble' — the idol-manufacturing process described with deliberate irony: 'vaychazeq charash et-tsaref, and the craftsman strengthened/encouraged the goldsmith.' The process: (1) hammering smooth (melaqqed pattish = the one smoothing with the hammer); (2) striking the anvil (holam patish = striking the anvil); (3) evaluating the solder ('it is good' = tov hu, the creation-evaluation of Gen 1); (4) fastening with nails so it cannot wobble (veleso yimot = and it will not topple). The irony: the craftsmen use the encouragement language of YHWH's service (chazaq = strengthen, tov = good) for their futile manufacturing project. The nail-fastening so the idol 'won't wobble' echoes Isaiah 40:20's 'idol that will not topple' — the craftsman's primary engineering challenge is structural stability for a supposed deity.
8: 'But you, Israel, my servant, Jacob, whom I have chosen, offspring of Abraham my friend' — the adversative pivot from the coastlands' idol-manufacturing (vv5-7) to Israel's identity: 've'attah Yisrael avdi, but you, Israel, my servant.' Three identity-designations: (1) 'my servant' (avdi = the one in covenant-service relationship, the 'eved YHWH); (2) 'Jacob, whom I have chosen' (Ya'aqov asher bechartikha = Jacob whom I have chosen); (3) 'offspring of Abraham my friend' (zera Avraham ohavi = seed of Abraham my lover/friend). The ohavi (my friend/one who loves me) = one of only three OT texts calling Abraham YHWH's 'friend': 2 Chronicles 20:7 ('Abraham your friend forever'), James 2:23 ('he was called God's friend'). The identity of Israel as 'Abraham's offspring' grounds the current election in the ancient patriarchal covenant — the choosing of Israel did not begin in the Sinai-generation but in Abraham's call (Gen 12).
9: 'You whom I took from the ends of the earth and called from its farthest regions, saying to you, You are my servant; I have chosen you and not rejected you' — the geographical scope of the divine call to Abraham: 'asher chelaztikhah miketsot ha'arets, whom I seized from the ends of the earth.' The chelaztikhah (I seized/took hold of = the forceful taking of someone by the arm, cf. Isaiah 42:6: 'I will take hold of your hand') applied to YHWH's call of Abraham from Ur of the Chaldeans (the 'ends of the earth' = Mesopotamia, the farthest known region from Canaan's perspective). 'Called from its farthest regions' (umiatsileha qera'tikha, from its distant corners I called you) = the call of Abraham in Genesis 12:1 ('go from your country, your people, your father's household'). The conclusion: 'I have chosen you and not rejected you' (bechartikha velo me'astikha, I chose you and did not spurn you). The divine non-rejection is the assurance for the exilic community: despite the exile (which could feel like rejection), the foundational choice stands.
10: 'Do not fear, for I am with you; do not be dismayed, for I am your God; I will strengthen you; I will help you; I will uphold you with my righteous right hand' — the quintessential comfort oracle of Second Isaiah: 'al-tira ki-imkha ani, do not fear, for I am with you.' The five divine promises (with Hebrew): (1) imkha ani = I am with you; (2) Elohekha ani = I am your God; (3) imtiztikhah = I will strengthen you; (4) azartikhah = I will help you; (5) tamakhtikha bimin tsidqi = I have upheld you by my right hand of righteousness. The chiastic structure: 'do not fear' / 'I am with you' // 'do not be dismayed' / 'I am your God' / [five promises]. The al-tira (do not fear) is the standard divine comfort-formula of the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 15:1; Isaiah 26:24; Isaiah 46:3) now given its fullest exposition: YHWH's presence (v10a), YHWH's identity as Israel's God (v10b), five active promises of support. The 'righteous right hand' (ymin tsidqi = right hand of my righteousness) = the hand that acts in accordance with YHWH's covenant-faithfulness.
11: 'See — all who are enraged against you will be put to shame and disgraced; those who oppose you will come to nothing and perish' — the assurance of enemies' failure: 'hen yevoshu veyikkalemu kol hacharim bekha, see, they will be shamed and disgraced — all who are enraged against you.' The yevoshu (shame = the public exposure of failure before an audience, the social-death of honor-shame culture). 'Those who oppose you will come to nothing and perish' (yihyu ka'ayin veyo'vedu anshei rivekha = they will be as nothing and perish, the men of your legal dispute). The 'come to nothing' (ka'ayin = like a nothing/non-being) returns to Isaiah 40:17's 'the nations are as nothing before him' — what is 'nothing' before YHWH becomes nothing when opposed to Israel under YHWH's protection. The legal language ('men of your dispute') continues the courtroom setting.
12: 'You will seek your adversaries but will not find them; those who make war against you will be as nothing at all' — the complete disappearance of Israel's enemies: 'tevaqshhem velo timtsa'em anshei matsutekha, you will seek them and not find them, the men who contend with you.' The seeking-without-finding = the total disappearance of the enemy, contrasting with YHWH being found when sought (Isaiah 55:6: 'seek the LORD while he may be found'). 'Those who make war against you will be as nothing at all' (yihyu ke'ayin uchephes anshei milchamtekha = they will be as nothing and non-existent, the men of your war). The double non-existence: ka'ayin (like nothing) and kephes (like nothing/empty = a stronger term). The exiles' enemies — Babylon's imperial power, the garrison troops, the administrative officials who humiliated them — will simply not be there.
13: 'For I, the LORD your God, hold your right hand; I am the one who says to you, Do not fear; I will help you' — the intimate divine hand-holding: 'ki ani YHWH Elohekha machaziq yeminekha, for I, YHWH your God, am grasping your right hand.' The machaziiq (grasping/holding firmly = from chazaq = to be strong, grip firmly, cf. Isaiah 42:6: 'I will take hold of your hand and keep you'). YHWH holds Israel's right hand — the same right hand that YHWH upholds with his own right hand (v10: 'I uphold you with my righteous right hand'). The two right hands: YHWH's right hand upholds Israel; YHWH grasps Israel's right hand. The double assurance: from above and alongside simultaneously. 'I am the one who says to you: Do not fear; I will help you' (ha'omer lekha al-tira ani azartikhah = the one saying to you: do not fear, I have helped you) — the promise of help given in the perfect tense of accomplished fact (azartikhah = I have already helped you) even as the exile continues.
14: 'Do not be afraid, O Jacob, you worm; you few men of Israel! I am the one who helps you, declares the LORD; your Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel' — the self-deprecating address that makes the comfort more astonishing: 'al-tiri tola'at Ya'aqov, do not be afraid, worm of Jacob.' The tola'at (worm/grub = the lowest form of animal life, a symbol of extreme vulnerability and weakness; Psalms 22:6: 'I am a worm, not a man, scorned by everyone and despised by the people'). 'You few men of Israel' (metei Yisrael = the men of Israel = the small, diminished remnant in Babylon) — addressing the community at its most depleted. The comfort is proportional to the address: the more worm-like the exiles feel, the more startling the divine promise. 'Your Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel' (go'alekha qedosh Yisrael = your kinsman-redeemer is the Holy One of Israel). The go'el (redeemer = the kinsman who pays the redemption price for a family member sold into debt-slavery, Leviticus 25:25-49; Ruth 4) applied to YHWH — the divine kinsman who will buy Israel back from Babylonian captivity at the cost of allowing Cyrus's conquests (Isaiah 43:3).
15: 'See — I will make you a new, sharp threshing sledge with many cutting edges; you will thresh the mountains and crush them, and reduce the hills to chaff' — the reversal of the worm into an instrument of power: 'hineh samtikha lemorag charuts chadash, behold I make you a threshing-sledge, a sharp new one.' The morag (threshing sledge = the heavy wooden sled, usually studded with sharp stones or iron pegs on its underside, dragged over harvested grain to separate kernel from stalk on the threshing floor). The 'worm of Jacob' becomes the threshing sledge. 'With many cutting edges' (ba'al piphiyot = master of mouths/edges = having many sharp edges on all sides). 'You will thresh the mountains and crush them' (tadush harim vetadoqem = you will thresh the mountains and reduce them to dust) — the mountains (the great empires: Babylon, Persia) reduced to chaff by YHWH's instrument Israel. The eschatological reversal: the humiliated become the instrument of judgment on the great.
16: 'You will winnow them, and the wind will carry them away and the gale will scatter them; you will rejoice in the LORD and glory in the Holy One of Israel' — the completion of the threshing metaphor: 'tizrem veruach tissaem useara yaphits otam, you will winnow them and wind will carry them and a gale will scatter them.' The three-stage grain-processing: (1) threshing (v15 = separating the grain from the stalk by the sledge); (2) winnowing (tizrem = the throwing of the crushed material into the wind so the light chaff blows away); (3) scattering by gale (seara yaphits = a whirlwind scatters what remains). The wind carrying away the chaff-enemies = the natural consequence of the divine harvest, not a further act of violence. 'You will rejoice in the LORD and glory in the Holy One of Israel' (vetattah bin YHWH veveqedosh Yisrael tithalal = you will rejoice in YHWH and in the Holy One of Israel you will boast) — the jubilation of the harvested people: the harvest of judgment on enemies produces the harvest of joy for Israel.
17: 'When the poor and the needy seek water and there is none, when their tongue is parched with thirst, I, the LORD, will answer them; I, the God of Israel, will not forsake them' — the water-promise oracle: 'haanevyonim ve'ha'evyonim mevaqshim mayim ve'ayin, the poor and the needy seek water and there is none.' The anivyim (poor = the economically vulnerable) and evyonim (the destitute needy = those who literally have nothing) = the exiles in their most desperate condition. The 'seeking water' may be literal (the desert journey of the return from Babylon) or metaphorical (seeking the life-sustaining presence of YHWH in exile). 'Their tongue parched with thirst' (nishsam tsama leshonam = their tongue dried up from thirst) — the physical extremity of dehydration as a figure for spiritual and social abandonment. 'I, the LORD, will answer them; I, the God of Israel, will not forsake them' (ani YHWH e'enehem Elohei Yisrael lo e'ezevem = I, YHWH, will answer them; the God of Israel, I will not abandon them) — the double divine identification with the double promise.
18: 'I will open rivers on the bare heights and fountains in the middle of the valleys; I will turn the wilderness into a pool of water and the dry ground into springs' — the wilderness-transformation oracle: 'eftach al-shefiim neharot uevetoch biqot ma'yanot, I will open on the bare heights rivers and in the midst of the valleys springs.' The shefiim (bare heights = the naked rocky ridges of the desert, where no moisture would normally be found) receive rivers; the biqot (valleys/ravines) receive springs. 'I will turn the wilderness into a pool of water' (asim midbar le'agam-mayim = I will make the wilderness a pool of water) and 'dry ground into springs' (ve'erets tsiyah lemo'tse'ei mayim = the arid land into water-sources). The new-Exodus transformation: the barren Syro-Arabian desert that the returning exiles must cross becomes a well-watered garden. The imagery draws on the Exodus wilderness (Exodus 17:6: water from the rock; Numbers 20:11: water from the rock again) but exceeds it: not just one miraculous spring but a landscape transformation.
19: 'I will plant in the wilderness cedar, acacia, myrtle, and olive trees; I will set in the desert cypress, plane tree, and pine together' — the seven-tree oracle of new creation: 'eten bamidbar erez shittah vahadds veets shemen, I will give/plant in the wilderness cedar, acacia, myrtle, and oil-tree.' The seven trees: (1) erez = cedar (the prestige timber of Lebanon, used for temples and palaces); (2) shittah = acacia (the desert-adapted hardwood, used for the tabernacle: Exod 25-27); (3) hadac = myrtle (the fragrant evergreen bush of Palestinian hills, used in Sukkoth: Nehemiah 8:15; Zechariah 1:8-11); (4) ets shemen = olive/oil-tree; (5) berosh = cypress; (6) tiddhar = plane tree (probably the elm or holm-oak); (7) te'ashshur = pine/box-tree. Seven trees = completeness. The planting in the wilderness where no trees grow = the reversal of curse (the barren wilderness as punishment for covenant-breaking becomes a garden through covenant-restoration). The acacia's inclusion specifically echoes the tabernacle — the new-Exodus includes a new place of divine dwelling.
20: 'So that they may see and know, consider and understand together, that the hand of the LORD has done this, and the Holy One of Israel has created it' — the purpose clause for all the wilderness-transformation oracles: 'lema'an yiru veyede'u veyasimu veyaskillu yachdav, so that they may see and know and consider and understand together.' The four cognitive verbs (see, know, consider, understand = re'oh, yadah, sum, sakhal) = a comprehensive understanding that moves from empirical observation (see) through knowledge to reflection and wisdom. 'That the hand of the LORD has done this' (ki yad-YHWH asthah zot = for the hand of YHWH has done this) and 'the Holy One of Israel has created it' (veqedosh Yisrael bera'ah). The water and trees are signs designed to produce recognition of YHWH — the new Exodus is missionary: it is calibrated to produce universal 'seeing and knowing.' The double identification (YHWH's hand + the Holy One of Israel) appears throughout the Cyrus-oracle sections, insisting that the redemption is specifically Israel's covenant-God's act, not a general providence.
21: 'Present your case, says the LORD; bring forward your strongest arguments, says the King of Jacob' — the divine court reconvenes after the Israel-comfort section (vv8-20): 'qarvu rivekhem yomar YHWH, bring near your case, says YHWH.' The riv (legal case/dispute = the technical term for the covenant lawsuit, the rib, appearing in Micah 6:1-2; Isaiah 1:18; Hosea 4:1; Jeremiah 2:9 as YHWH's legal dispute with Israel; here YHWH invites the idols' legal case). 'Bring forward your strongest arguments' (habiyu atsumotelkhem, bring your strongest proofs) = 'atstumot = the strongest, most powerful arguments. 'Says the King of Jacob' (yomar melek Ya'aqov) — YHWH's kingship over Jacob/Israel is the legal standing from which he adjudicates the case. The invitation is genuinely open: YHWH does not refuse the evidence of the idols but invites their best case.
22: 'Let them come forward and tell us what will happen; let them declare the former things, what they were, so we may consider them and know their outcome; or announce to us what is to come' — the two-part test of true deity: prediction of the past and prediction of the future. (1) 'Let them declare the former things, what they were' (veharishonot mah hennah yaggidu = and the former things, what they are, let them tell) — can the idols explain the meaning of past history? The rishonut (former things = the historical events of the past, specifically the Exodus and the Assyrian crisis) as interpreted events: can the idols explain what YHWH has already done? (2) 'Or announce to us what is to come' (o haba'ot hashmi'unu = or the coming things, let them make us hear) — can they predict the future? The Babylonian divination system (astrology, hepatoscopy, extispicy) claimed to do exactly this. YHWH's courtroom challenge is to the entire Babylonian religious-intellectual system: can your methods actually predict what will happen?
23: 'Tell us what is to come in the future, so that we may know you are gods. Do good or do evil — do something — so that we may see it and stand in awe together' — the challenge reaching its maximum: 'haggidu ha'atidot le'achor, tell us the things to come, for the future.' The desperate offer: 'asu-tov o-hare'u, do good or do evil.' The gods are invited to do anything at all — good or bad — just something observable and testable. 'So that we may see it and stand in awe together' (venire'eh venirad yachdav = and we will see and fear together) — the standard response to genuine divine action is fear (Genesis 28:17: 'how awesome is this place'; Exodus 14:31: 'Israel saw the great power of YHWH'). The idols cannot produce this fear because they cannot act. The challenge is ultimately performative: not doctrine but demonstration.
24: 'See — you are nothing and your work amounts to nothing; whoever chooses you is an abomination' — the verdict against the idols: 'hen-attem me'ayin ufaalekhem me'afah, behold you are from nothing and your work from nothing.' The categorical verdict: the idols are not just weak but ontologically nothing (me'ayin = from the nothing of Isaiah 40:17; me'afah = from the breath/nothing). 'Whoever chooses you is an abomination' (toevah yivchar bakhem = an abomination, whoever chooses you). The toa'evah (abomination = the strongest negative evaluative term in Hebrew, used for religious idolatry in Deuteronomy 7:25; Isaiah 13:14; Isaiah 17:1; 27:15 and for sexual ethics in Leviticus 18:22; Isaiah 20:13; and for false weights in Proverbs 11:1). The choice of an idol is not merely unwise but abominable — the strongest possible condemnation. The verdict of the divine courtroom after a fair hearing in which the idols could not produce evidence.
25: 'I have stirred up one from the north, and he comes; from the east he comes, calling on my name; he will trample rulers as if they were mortar, as a potter treads clay' — the Cyrus-oracle resumed: 'ha'iroti mitsafon vayato mimizrach-shemesh yiqra vishmi, I stirred up one from the north, and he came; from the rising of the sun he calls on my name.' The double direction: from the north (Cyrus's ally Media is north of Babylon) and from the east (Persia is east). 'Calling on my name' (yiqra vishmi = calls by my name) = Cyrus acknowledged YHWH in the Cyrus Cylinder (538 BCE), where he attributes his conquests to divine sanction, and in Ezra 1:2: 'the LORD, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth.' 'He will trample rulers as if they were mortar' (veyavo saganim kemoh-homer = he will come on governors as if on clay) = the vivid image of boots trampling the clay of the battlefield. 'As a potter treads clay' (ukhmoh yotser yirmas-tit = as a potter treads the wet clay) — the same material transformed by Cyrus's feet from obstacle to raw material.
26: 'Who declared this from the beginning, so we could know? Who foretold it beforehand, so we could say, He is right? Not one declared it; not one announced it; not one heard you say it' — the proof-from-prediction argument applied to Cyrus: 'mi-higgid mero'sh venede'ah, who declared from the beginning and we would know?' The retrospective question: which deity announced Cyrus before Cyrus appeared? The answer: 'en maggid, en mashmi'ah, en shome'a imroteikhem, no one declared, no one proclaimed, no one heard your words.' The triple negation: no declaration, no proclamation, no audience. The Babylonian diviners did not predict Cyrus; their extensive omen-literature and astrological system gave them no advance warning of the Persian conquest. Only YHWH predicted it — through Isaiah in the 8th century, naming Cyrus 150 years before his birth (Isaiah 44:28; Isaiah 45:1).
27: 'I was the first to say to Zion, Look — here they are! And I give Jerusalem a messenger of good news' — YHWH's self-identification as the first herald: 'rishon letsiyon hinneh hinnam, the first to Zion: behold, here they are!' YHWH himself is the first herald who announced to Zion that the return was coming — before any human messenger arrived. 'I give Jerusalem a messenger of good news' (veliYerushalayim mevasser etten = and to Jerusalem I give a bringer-of-good-news). The mevasser (herald of good news = the same root as the gospel, euangelion; cf. 40:9 where Zion/Jerusalem is the mevasser). Here YHWH gives the city a herald — the announcement of the return is YHWH's own first word to his city. The verse contrasts with the idol-diviners of v26 (who could say nothing) and YHWH who spoke first.
28: 'For I looked, and there was no one; among them there was no counselor; when I asked them, not one could answer' — the divine inspection of the idol-assembly for a valid counselor: 've'ere ve'en ish, and I looked and there was no man.' YHWH surveyed the assembly of the nations' gods and their representatives and found: 'among them there was no counselor' (ume'eleh ein yo'ets = and from these there is no counselor). The yo'ets (counselor = the political-military advisor, the same term as the messianic title in Isaiah 9:6: 'Wonderful Counselor'). 'When I asked them, not one could answer' (vesh'al otam vayyashivu davar = and I asked them and they returned a word) — the idols cannot answer divine inquiry. The comparison: the nations' gods have no counselors who can answer divine questioning; YHWH himself is the Wonderful Counselor (Isaiah 9:6) whose counsel is perfect (Isaiah 25:1: 'your counsel of old is faithful and true').
29: 'See — they are all emptiness; their works come to nothing; their cast images are just wind and futility' — the final verdict of the divine courtroom over idols: 'hen kullam aven, behold they are all emptiness.' The aven (emptiness/trouble/iniquity = the comprehensive word for the worthlessness of idolatry; used in Hosea 4:15 for Bethel; Amos 5:5 for the sanctuary at Gilgal: 'Beth-aven, house of wickedness'). 'Their works come to nothing' (ma'aseihem efes = their works are nothingness) — the same efes as Isaiah 40:17 ('the nations are as nothing'). 'Their cast images are just wind and futility' (ruach vetohu nesikheihem = wind and chaos are their cast-images). The tohu (chaos = the Genesis 1:2 pre-creation void) applied to the molten images — they have achieved the anti-creation. YHWH's work is creation; idol-worship is the return to chaos and nothingness.
